patient_description: I first noticed something was off about six months ago when my right hand started shaking on its own while I was just sitting on the couch watching TV. It’s like a trembling that goes away the second I reach to pick up my coffee mug, but as soon as I rest my hand back in my lap, it starts up again. Lately, everything just feels like it’s running in slow motion. Getting out of my favorite armchair feels like an absolute chore, like my body is moving through wet cement. My husband also pointed out that I don't seem to smile or show much expression on my face anymore—he asked if I was depressed, but I’m not, my face just feels sort of frozen or stiff. On top of that, my handwriting has become so tiny and cramped that I can barely read my own grocery lists. I've also started shuffling my feet when I walk, and I'm terrified of falling because my balance feels so shaky.
doctor_description: Subjective: The patient is a 67-year-old right-handed individual who presents with a progressive, insidious onset of motor difficulties over the past several months. Chief complaints include a persistent resting tremor in the right upper extremity, described as a "pill-rolling" motion, which decreases with voluntary activity. The patient reports significant, generalized slowness of movement (bradykinesia), experiencing marked difficulty when initiating movements such as rising from a seated position. Family members have noted a reduction in facial expressiveness and a softer, quieter speaking voice. The patient also reports micrographic changes in handwriting and a sense of imbalance, noting a few near-falls when turning. Objective: - Mental Status: Alert, oriented, normal affect, though exhibiting hypomimia (masked facies). - Cranial Nerves: Grossly intact. Infrequent blinking noted. - Motor System: Bilateral upper extremity rigidity, more pronounced on the right side, exhibiting a distinct "cogwheel" quality upon passive manipulation. - Coordination: Marked bradykinesia demonstrated during rapid alternating movements, finger-tapping, and heel-to-shin testing, with progressive decrements in amplitude. - Gait/Posture: Posture is stooped. Gait is characterized by a slow, shuffling stride with reduced right-arm swing. A positive pull test confirms moderate postural instability. Assessment: The clinical presentation is highly indicative of a progressive neurodegenerative extrapyramidal disorder. The patient exhibits the classic triad of asymmetric resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia, alongside secondary features including postural instability, micrographic handwriting, and hypomimia.
